segments.title: A Shinkansen terminus built for future capacity
segments.summary: At Hakodate’s Shinkansen terminus, Clarke points to unused platform capacity as an example of rail infrastructure planned with expansion in mind.
segments.body: Clarke begins his journey south from Hakodate by looking closely at the station’s role as the current end of the Shinkansen line. He describes the building as functional rather than ornamental, but argues that its design works because it is shaped around the railway’s needs. The visible end of track and provision for another platform lead him to a wider observation about infrastructure planning. In his view, the station shows Japanese infrastructure being built with future growth and extension in mind, while still acknowledging that no human organization plans perfectly.

evidence.note: Clarke identifies the Hakodate terminus, its end of track, provision for an additional platform, and his conclusion about future-minded infrastructure.
